symbols.name: \operatorname{Id}^{\epsilon}_P(\delta_Q)
symbols.role: structurally compatible values of the target

novel.justification: This is a deliberately saturated discrete-IV design regime: every latent-resistance cutpoint is represented by an instrument arm within each covariate cell, as can occur in randomized encouragement designs with discretized compliance scores. It is imposed transparently because cell-by-cell response-type recovery is the finite-grid object studied here.

The restriction encodes first-order stochastic worsening of untreated potential-outcome ranks with latent resistance. In settings where resistance is a disadvantage index, it is a transparent finite-grid sensitivity restriction whose inequalities can be reported and relaxed separately.

The treated-outcome analogue is credible under a common latent disadvantage index. Its finite set of rank inequalities keeps the maintained selection restriction observable as a sensitivity specification rather than an opaque copula choice.

A lower bound on pooled potential-outcome rank association formalizes calibrated limits on latent rank discordance. Validation data, benchmark copulas, or an elicited dependence scenario can determine the baseline and the relaxation scale.

assumptions.condition: W_1,\ldots,W_n are i.i.d. with law P, and the F cross-fitting folds form a partition with n_f/n\to\gamma_f\in(0,1) for every f.

novel.justification: standard: uniform consistency on a finite propensity grid

assumptions.condition: In the representative-cutpoint reduced factual parameterization, every nonstructurally-zero recovered row and column margin is strictly positive and every rank-shape inequality holds strictly.

novel.justification: standard: relative-interiority condition for finite-dimensional constrained estimation

assumptions.condition: The full-row-rank reduced LP defining \overline S_-(P) has a unique primal optimizer, a unique reduced dual optimizer, a nonsingular active-constraint basis, and strict complementarity.
The influence function obtained by composing the reduced-LP envelope gradient with the finite-cell observed-data scores has variance \sigma^2 satisfying 0<\sigma^2<\infty.

definitions.name: rank-structured factual response polytope
definitions.construction: \mathcal R_\infty(P)=\{r\in\mathbb R_+^{M\times K\times J\times J}:\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}=\lambda_k\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M,\ k=1,\ldots,K;\ A_m^Pr=(b_{d\ell hm})_{d\in\{0,1\},\ell=1,\ldots,L,h=1,\ldots,J}\ \forall m=1,\ldots,M;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^t\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J;\ \lambda_{k+1}^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{m,k+1,i,j}\le\lambda_k^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^tr_{mkij}\ \forall m,k<K,t<J\}.

definitions.name: compatible observed-law domain
definitions.construction: \mathcal P_\epsilon=\{P\in\mathcal P(\mathcal W):\alpha_m>0\ \forall m;\ \pi_{\ell m}>0\ \forall\ell,m;\ \{p_{m\ell}:\ell=1,\ldots,L\}=\{q_k:k=0,\ldots,K\}\ \forall m;\ \mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\ne\varnothing\}.

definitions.name: compatible generalized-Roy law class
definitions.construction: \mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P)=\{Q:Q_W=P;\ Y=DY(1)+(1-D)Y(0)\ Q\text{-a.s.};\ (Y(0),Y(1),U)\perp Z\mid X;\ D=1\{U\le p_{m\ell}\}\ Q\text{-a.s. on }\{Z=z_\ell,X=x_m\}\ \forall\ell,m;\ U\mid X=x_m\sim\operatorname{Uniform}[0,1]\ \forall m;\ r^Q\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\}.

definitions.name: association-frontier handle
definitions.construction: Handle: use the one-array response-type polytope \mathcal R_\epsilon(P), prove the converse by constructing a common generalized-Roy law from every feasible array, and use parametric-LP duality to compare the sign-reversing face \{r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P):\Delta(r)\le0\} with the association floor S(r)\ge\kappa_0-e.

definitions.name: dual inference handle
definitions.construction: Handle: on each training fold, round \widehat p^{(-f)}_{m\ell} to its nearest grid point q_s, aggregate or select one arm for each represented cutpoint, and estimate \alpha_m and the arm-specific factual outcome cells. Calibrate these cells to the fixed-threshold Roy factual-law polytope (equivalently, recover the cellwise transport margins by adjacent differences) and express that polytope with a fixed full-row-rank equality system obtained by deleting redundant marginal and duplicate-arm equations. Solve the reduced primal-dual LP for \overline S_-(P), form its cross-fitted one-step estimator by composing the unique reduced dual/envelope gradient with held-out finite-cell residuals, and set \widehat\epsilon^\dagger=(\kappa_0-\widehat{\overline S}_-)_+. The rounded threshold has no first-order score on the correct-classification event. At primal or dual ties, estimate the active-face directional derivative of the reduced LP and apply a directional numerical-delta/bootstrap procedure.

statements.statement: For every P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon, under ass:consistency, ass:iv-exogeneity, ass:threshold-selection, ass:uniform-resistance, ass:rank-shape-y0, ass:rank-shape-y1, and ass:rank-association-floor, Q\in\mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P) implies r^Q\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P) and \delta_Q=\Delta(r^Q). Conversely, each r\in\mathcal R_\epsilon(P) equals r^Q for a Q\in\mathfrak Q_\epsilon(P).
statements.justification: The result makes the finite constrained-OT program a structural object by establishing both factual compatibility and a common-law pasting construction.
statements.gap: Tan, Blanchet, and Syrgkanis (2026) reduce product costs, while the prior response-type failure in the gap brief records that cellwise feasible tables do not by themselves yield a generalized-Roy law.
statements.consumer: It permits gain-band conclusions to be read as statements about one joint potential-outcome law rather than an outer relaxation of observed cells.

statements.statement: Under ass:cutpoint-saturation with K=1, for every m and every instrument arm with p_{m\ell}=1, [A_m^Pr]_{1\ell h}=\sum_{i=1}^Jr_{m1ih}; for every arm with p_{m\ell}=0, [A_m^Pr]_{0\ell h}=\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{m1hj}. Thus the treated block is the Y(1)=y_h marginal and the untreated block is the Y(0)=y_h marginal, exactly as required by consistency.
statements.justification: This hand reduction audits the indexing of the factual map in the smallest threshold model.
statements.gap: It is a corrected normalization check, not a claimed advance over published work.
statements.consumer: It prevents the response-type LP from silently exchanging treated and untreated outcome marginals in implementation.
statements.proof_tex:
Proof. Suppose K=1. Then the resistance partition has the single cell I_1=[q_0,q_1]=[0,1], with q_0=0 and q_1=1. By ass:cutpoint-saturation, for every covariate cell m the represented treatment thresholds are exactly {q_0,q_1}={0,1}. Fix m, h, and an instrument arm ell. If p_{m ell}=1, def:response-polytope gives

[A_m^P r]_{1 ell h} = sum_{k: q_k <= p_{m ell}} sum_{i=1}^J r_{m k i h}.

Because K=1 and q_1=1=p_{m ell}, the outer index set is the singleton {1}. Therefore

[A_m^P r]_{1 ell h} = sum_{i=1}^J r_{m 1 i h}.

The third response-type index records Y(0), while the fourth records Y(1); summing over i thus leaves the Y(1)=y_h marginal.

If p_{m ell}=0, def:response-polytope instead gives

[A_m^P r]_{0 ell h} = sum_{k: q_{k-1} >= p_{m ell}} sum_{j=1}^J r_{m k h j}.

Again K=1, and q_{1-1}=q_0=0=p_{m ell}, so the outer index set is the singleton {1}. Consequently

[A_m^P r]_{0 ell h} = sum_{j=1}^J r_{m 1 h j}.

Summing over the fourth index leaves the Y(0)=y_h marginal. Thus the always-treated arm p_{m ell}=1 reveals the treated-potential-outcome marginal, whereas the never-treated arm p_{m ell}=0 reveals the untreated-potential-outcome marginal, precisely the indexing dictated by Y=DY(1)+(1-D)Y(0). QED.

statements.statement: Under ass:cutpoint-saturation, if c_{ij}=\xi_i+\zeta_j for fixed loadings (\xi_i)_{i=1}^J and (\zeta_j)_{j=1}^J, then \Delta(r) is constant on \mathcal R_\infty(P). Hence, for every \epsilon with P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon, \delta_L(P,\epsilon)=\delta_U(P,\epsilon).
statements.justification: This is the exact separable-cost reduction that distinguishes the gain-band's joint-law content from marginal PRTE functionals.
statements.gap: It recovers the product-cost regime used by Tan, Blanchet, and Syrgkanis (2026) and is only a nesting check.
statements.consumer: It tells applied users when marginal PRTE methods are sufficient and when the common-coupling frontier is necessary.
statements.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Fix an observed law $P$ satisfying 	extnormal{ass:cutpoint-saturation}, and fix $r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P)$. For $m=1,\ldots,M$, $k=1,\ldots,K$, and the appropriate outcome indices, define the two cellwise marginals
\[
R_{mki}(r):=\sum_{j=1}^J r_{mkij},\qquad C_{mkj}(r):=\sum_{i=1}^J r_{mkij}.
\]
For every $m$ and every $s\in\{0,\ldots,K\}$, cutpoint saturation permits us to choose an instrument index $\ell_m(s)$ such that $p_{m,\ell_m(s)}=q_s$. Put
\[
B^1_{msj}:=b_{1,\ell_m(s),j,m},\qquad B^0_{msi}:=b_{0,\ell_m(s),i,m}.
\]
The factual equalities $A_m^P r=b$ in 	extnormal{def:response-polytope}, together with the strict ordering $q_0<\cdots<q_K$, imply
\[
B^1_{msj}=[A_m^Pr]_{1,\ell_m(s),j}
 =\sum_{v:q_v\le q_s}\sum_{i=1}^Jr_{mvij}
 =\sum_{v=1}^{s}C_{mvj}(r),
\tag{1}
\]
and
\[
B^0_{msi}=[A_m^Pr]_{0,\ell_m(s),i}
 =\sum_{v:q_{v-1}\ge q_s}\sum_{j=1}^Jr_{mvij}
 =\sum_{v=s+1}^{K}R_{mvi}(r).
\tag{2}
\]
Here and below an empty sum is zero, so (1)--(2) include the boundary cutpoints $s=0$ and $s=K$. Taking adjacent differences gives, for every $k=1,\ldots,K$,
\[
C_{mkj}(r)=B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j},\qquad
R_{mki}(r)=B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki}.
\tag{3}
\]
The right-hand sides of (3) are functionals of the fixed observed law $P$ and do not depend on $r$. Consequently, every array in $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ has the same cellwise $Y(0)$ and $Y(1)$ marginals. (If more than one arm represents a cutpoint, feasibility of $r$ makes the corresponding cumulative factual margins agree, so the choice of $\ell_m(s)$ is immaterial.)

Now impose $c_{ij}=\xi_i+\zeta_j$. Using finite-sum rearrangement and (3),
\begin{align*}
\Delta(r)
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k\sum_{i=1}^J\sum_{j=1}^J(\xi_i+\zeta_j)r_{mkij}\\
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k
\left\{\sum_{i=1}^J\xi_iR_{mki}(r)+\sum_{j=1}^J\zeta_jC_{mkj}(r)\right\}\\
&=\sum_{m=1}^M\sum_{k=1}^K\alpha_m d_k\left\{
\sum_{i=1}^J\xi_i(B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki})
+\sum_{j=1}^J\zeta_j(B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j})\right\}.
\tag{4}
\end{align*}
Expression (4) depends only on $P$ and the fixed loadings and weights, proving that $\Delta$ is constant on $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$.

Finally, if $P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon$, then $\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)$ is nonempty by 	extnormal{def:feasible-observed-law-domain}, and $\mathcal R_\epsilon(P)\subseteq\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ by 	extnormal{def:association-response-polytope}. Thus the minimum and maximum in 	extnormal{def:policy-endpoints} are taken over a nonempty set on which $\Delta$ has the same value. Therefore
\[
\delta_L(P,\epsilon)=\delta_U(P,\epsilon).
\]
\end{proof}

statements.statement: If a finite-dimensional linear program has an optimal solution, then its dual has an optimal solution and the primal and dual optimal values are equal.

source.verbatim_statement: If a linear programming problem has an optimal solution, so does its dual, and the respective optimal costs are equal.

attestation.note: Transcribed from Bertsimas and Tsitsiklis, Introduction to Linear Optimization, Theorem 4.4.

statements.statement: If $X_1,X_2,\ldots$ are i.i.d. random vectors in $\mathbb R^d$ with mean $\mu$ and finite covariance matrix $\Sigma$, then $\sqrt n(\bar X_n-\mu)\Rightarrow N_d(0,\Sigma)$.

attestation.note: Checked against van der Vaart, Asymptotic Statistics, Example 2.18; the DOI is supplied as the stable source handle.

statements.statement: Let $P$ belong to $\mathcal P_e$ for some $e$ and choose $\ell_m(s)$ with $p_{m,\ell_m(s)}=q_s$. Define $B^d_{msh}=b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}$, $u_{mki}=\alpha_m(B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki})$, and $v_{mkj}=\alpha_m(B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j})$. The change of variables $t_{mkij}=\alpha_mr_{mkij}$ bijects $\mathcal R_\infty(P)$ with the nonnegative transport arrays having row margins $u$, column margins $v$, and the fixed rank restrictions already encoded by these margins. Under $\Delta(r)\le0$, the objective and sign constraint become $\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. Consequently $\overline S_-(P)=V(\theta(P))$ for the fixed-coefficient reduced LP stated in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference.
statements.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Fix $m$ and $s$. The factual equations at a representative arm give
\[
B^1_{msj}=\sum_{k=1}^{s}\sum_i r_{mkij},\qquad
B^0_{msi}=\sum_{k=s+1}^{K}\sum_j r_{mkij}.
\tag{1}
\]
Adjacent differences therefore identify the conditional transport margins
\[
\sum_jr_{mkij}=B^0_{m,k-1,i}-B^0_{mki},\qquad
\sum_ir_{mkij}=B^1_{mkj}-B^1_{m,k-1,j}.
\tag{2}
\]
If multiple arms represent $q_s$, existence of one feasible response array forces their factual blocks to agree with the common cumulative sums in (1), so the representative is immaterial. Conversely, any nonnegative collection of cellwise couplings with margins (2) reproduces every factual equation by summing the adjacent differences. The rank-shape inequalities depend only on the row and column margins, so they are then automatic. Since $\alpha_m>0$, multiplication by $\alpha_m$ is a bijection. It changes (2) into the margins $u,v$ in the statement and gives
\[
S(r)=\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij},\qquad
\Delta(r)=\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}.
\tag{3}
\]
Deleting one redundant row-or-column equality in each transport cell does not alter its feasible arrays and makes the equality system full row rank. Maximizing the first expression in (3) subject to the second being nonpositive is exactly the program defining $V(\theta(P))$, proving the claim.
\end{proof}

statements.statement:
For a finite fixed-coefficient LP $V(	heta)=\max\{s^Tt:Bt=	heta,\ g^Tt\le0,\ t\ge0\}$ with $B$ full row rank, $V$ is finite, concave, continuous and piecewise affine on the relative interior of its feasible right-hand-side domain. Its dual is $V(	heta)=\min\{y^T	heta:B^Ty+
u g\ge s,
u\ge0\}$, and $V'_	heta(h)=\min_{(y,
u)\in\mathcal D^*(	heta)}y^Th$. If the optimal active basis and reduced dual are unique, nonsingular, and strictly complementary, then $V$ is locally affine with gradient equal to the unique $y$. The analogous minimization LP with a scalar right-hand-side relaxation is convex and piecewise affine, and its derivative equals the signed unique multiplier.

Under finite-support positivity, i.i.d. stable cross-fitting, consistent nearest-grid cutpoint classification, and factual-margin relative interiority, the calibrated augmented estimator $\widehat\theta=TC(\widetilde\alpha,\widetilde\eta)$ defined in thm:orthogonal-sign-radius-inference satisfies $\sqrt n(\widehat\theta-\theta)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)\Rightarrow N(0,\Sigma_\theta)$, where $\psi_\theta=TC\psi_\chi$ and $\psi_\chi$ is given by equations (3)--(4) in that theorem's proof. The estimating equation for every $\eta$ coordinate is Neyman orthogonal with respect to its conditional-cell and instrument-propensity nuisances.
statements.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Because the grid is finite and strictly separated, uniform consistency of each training-fold propensity estimator makes nearest-grid classification correct in every cell with probability $1-o(1)$. Work on this event. Training empirical conditional-cell and instrument probabilities are root-$n$ consistent because their denominators have probabilities $\alpha_m\pi_{\ell m}>0$ and every training sample has order $n$.

For one coordinate suppress indices and write $b=P(A\mid Z=z_\ell,X=x_m)$ and $\pi=P(Z=z_\ell\mid X=x_m)$. The held-out augmented summand is
\[
m(W;\widetilde b,\widetilde\pi)=1\{X=x_m\}\widetilde b+
\frac{1\{X=x_m,Z=z_\ell\}}{\widetilde\pi}
(1\{A\}-\widetilde b).
\tag{1}
\]
Its expectation is $\alpha_m[\widetilde b+(\pi/\widetilde\pi)(b-\widetilde b)]$. At $(b,\pi)$ the derivatives with respect to both nuisance arguments are zero, and the exact bias is
\[
\alpha_m(\widetilde b-b)(1-\pi/\widetilde\pi),
\tag{2}
\]
a product of two $O_P(n^{-1/2})$ errors. Conditional on each training sample, the centered held-out average has the same first-order summand as (1) evaluated at truth; fixed dimension and bounded inverse propensities make replacement of estimated nuisances $o_P(n^{-1/2})$. Summing the folds with weights $n_f/n$ gives
\[
\sqrt n(\widetilde\chi-\chi)=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\chi(W_r)+o_P(1).
\tag{3}
\]
The formulas for $\psi_\chi$ follow by subtracting the mean of (1), while the empirical covariate mass has score $1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m$. The calibration $C$ and adjacent-difference operator $T$ are fixed linear maps on the correct-classification event and $C\chi=\chi$, so applying $TC$ to (3) gives the asserted expansion. Its summands are bounded finite-dimensional vectors; the multivariate i.i.d. CLT yields the Gaussian limit. Strict positivity of all nonstructural recovered margins and strict rank inequalities keep the calibrated estimator in the same relative face with probability tending to one.
\end{proof}

statements.statement: If $r_n(\widehat\theta_n-\theta_0)\Rightarrow\mathbb G_0$ and $\phi$ is Hadamard directionally differentiable at $\theta_0$ tangentially to the support of the tight limit, then $r_n\{\phi(\widehat\theta_n)-\phi(\theta_0)\}=\phi'_{\theta_0}(r_n(\widehat\theta_n-\theta_0))+o_P(1)\Rightarrow\phi'_{\theta_0}(\mathbb G_0)$.

attestation.note: Checked against Fang and Santos (2019), Review of Economic Studies 86, Theorem 2.1.

statements.statement: When a bootstrap consistently estimates the tight limit of $r_n(\widehat\theta_n-\theta_0)$ and an estimated directional derivative converges uniformly on compact direction sets to $\phi'_{\theta_0}$, its composition with the bootstrap consistently estimates the law of $\phi'_{\theta_0}(\mathbb G_0)$. In finite dimension, the numerical derivative $[\phi(\widehat\theta_n+s_nh)-\phi(\widehat\theta_n)]/s_n$ has this property when $s_n\downarrow0$ and $r_ns_n\to\infty$.

source.locator: Theorem 3.2, equation (25), and Supplementary Lemma S.3.8

attestation.note: Checked against Fang and Santos (2019), Theorem 3.2 and their discussion of the numerical derivative in equation (25), which cites Supplementary Lemma S.3.8.

statements.statement: For every $P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon$, the structural identified set is exactly $\operatorname{Id}^{\epsilon}_P(\delta_Q)=[\delta_L(P,\epsilon),\delta_U(P,\epsilon)]$. Fix an observed law $P$ belonging to $\mathcal P_e$ for at least one $e\ge0$. If $\mathcal R^-_\infty(P)\ne\varnothing$, then $\epsilon^\dagger(P)=(\kappa_0-\overline S_-(P))_+$. On $E_P=\{e\ge0:P\in\mathcal P_e\}$, the map $e\mapsto\delta_L(P,e)$ is finite, nonincreasing, convex, continuous relative to $E_P$, and piecewise affine. At every relative-interior point $e$ at which the association-floor dual multiplier is unique, it is differentiable and $\delta_L'(P,e)=-\mu_e$. If additionally $P\in\mathcal P_0$, $\delta_L(P,0)>0$, $\overline S_-(P)<\kappa_0$, and $\min_{r\in\mathcal R_\infty(P)}\Delta(r)<0$, then $\delta_L(P,e)>0$ for $0\le e<\epsilon^\dagger(P)$, $\delta_L(P,\epsilon^\dagger(P))\le0$, and for every $e>\epsilon^\dagger(P)$ there is $Q_e\in\mathfrak Q_e(P)$ with $\delta_{Q_e}<0$.

Let $P$ satisfy ass:covariate-cell-positivity, ass:instrument-overlap, ass:cutpoint-saturation, ass:iid-crossfit-sampling, ass:cutpoint-estimator-consistency, ass:factual-margin-relative-interiority, and ass:positive-sign-radius. Select in each $(m,s)$ cell the least instrument arm whose cross-fitted propensity rounds to $q_s$; estimate its conditional $(Y,D)$ cells and instrument propensity on the training sample. Form the cross-fitted augmented estimators $\widetilde\eta_{dshm}=n^{-1}\sum_f\sum_{r\in I_f}[1\{X_r=x_m\}\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m}+1\{X_r=x_m,Z_r=z_{\widehat\ell_m(s)}\}(1\{Y_r=y_h,D_r=d\}-\widehat b^{(-f)}_{d,\widehat\ell_m(s),h,m})/\widehat\pi^{(-f)}_{\widehat\ell_m(s),m}]$ and $\widetilde\alpha_m=n^{-1}\sum_r1\{X_r=x_m\}$. Project $(\widetilde\alpha,\widetilde\eta)$ onto the affine constraints $\sum_h\eta_{1shm}=\alpha_mq_s$ and $\sum_h\eta_{0shm}=\alpha_m(1-q_s)$, and take adjacent differences $u_{mki}=\eta_{0,k-1,i,m}-\eta_{0,k,i,m}$ and $v_{mkj}=\eta_{1,k,j,m}-\eta_{1,k-1,j,m}$; call the resulting unconditional-margin vector $\widehat\theta$. Let $V(\theta)$ be the reduced fixed-coefficient LP $\max_{t\ge0}\sum_{mkij}\rho_i\rho_jt_{mkij}$ subject to $\sum_jt_{mkij}=u_{mki}$, $\sum_it_{mkij}=v_{mkj}$ (with redundant equalities deleted), and $\sum_{mkij}d_kc_{ij}t_{mkij}\le0$. Define $\widehat\epsilon^\dagger=(\kappa_0-V(\widehat\theta))_+$. Under ass:sign-lp-strong-regularity and ass:sign-radius-influence-variance, $\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)\Rightarrow N(0,\sigma^2)$ with influence function $-y_*^\top\psi_\theta(W)$, where $y_*$ is the unique reduced equality-dual optimizer and $\psi_\theta$ is the calibrated adjacent-difference finite-cell score; the plug-in score variance is consistent and the usual Wald interval has asymptotic coverage $1-\alpha$. Without primal or dual uniqueness, if the true margin vector is in the relative interior of the sign-LP feasible right-hand-side domain, the map $\Phi(\theta)=(\kappa_0-V(\theta))_+$ is Hadamard directionally differentiable. For $s_n\downarrow0$ with $\sqrt n s_n\to\infty$, the Gaussian-multiplier numerical derivative $[\Phi(\widehat\theta+s_n\mathbb G_n^*)-\Phi(\widehat\theta)]/s_n$ consistently estimates the law of $\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)$; confidence sets obtained from its absolute-value quantiles are asymptotically valid at continuity points of the limiting quantile. The relative-interior qualification is necessary to exclude the boundary at which the sign-reversal LP becomes infeasible and the finite radius can jump to infinity.
statements.proof_tex:
\begin{proof}
Let $\ell_m(s)=\min\{\ell:p_{m\ell}=q_s\}$. The minimum cutpoint spacing is positive. Therefore ass:cutpoint-estimator-consistency implies that, simultaneously over the finite set of folds and cells, nearest-grid rounding selects every true cutpoint class and hence $\widehat\ell_m(s)=\ell_m(s)$ with probability tending to one. On this event the estimated-threshold procedure equals its oracle-classification version exactly; equality on events of probability $1-o(1)$ preserves every asserted weak limit.

Define $\eta_{dshm}=\alpha_m b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}$. After deleting redundant equations, let $C$ denote the fixed Euclidean projection onto
\[
\sum_h\eta_{1shm}=\alpha_mq_s,\qquad
\sum_h\eta_{0shm}=\alpha_m(1-q_s),
\tag{1}
\]
and let $T$ be the fixed adjacent-difference map in the statement. The population vector $\chi=(\alpha,\eta)$ obeys (1), and lem:orthogonal-margin-expansion gives
\[
\sqrt n(\widehat\theta-\theta)
=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)
\Rightarrow G\sim N(0,\Sigma_\theta),
\qquad \psi_\theta=TC\psi_\chi.
\tag{2}
\]
The raw score used here is explicit:
\[
\psi_{\alpha_m}(W)=1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m,
\tag{3}
\]
\[
\psi_{\eta_{dshm}}(W)
=b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}(1\{X=x_m\}-\alpha_m)
+\frac{1\{X=x_m,Z=z_{\ell_m(s)}\}}{\pi_{\ell_m(s),m}}
\bigl(1\{Y=y_h,D=d\}-b_{d,\ell_m(s),h,m}\bigr).
\tag{4}
\]
The augmentation is Neyman orthogonal. Indeed, if $\widetilde b,\widetilde\pi$ replace the true nuisances in the summand defining $\widetilde\eta$, its expectation is
\[
\alpha_m\left[\widetilde b+
\frac{\pi}{\widetilde\pi}(b-\widetilde b)\right].
\tag{5}
\]
Both first derivatives of (5) with respect to $\widetilde b$ and $\widetilde\pi$ vanish at $(b,\pi)$. Finite support and positive cell probabilities make the training empirical nuisances root-$n$ consistent, so the product remainder in (5) is $O_P(n^{-1})=o_P(n^{-1/2})$. This proves the orthogonal expansion rather than assuming it. Relative interiority ensures that the calibrated adjacent-difference margins remain positive and satisfy the strict rank inequalities with probability tending to one.

By lem:unconditional-margin-lp-reduction, $V(\theta)=\overline S_-(P)$. Hence the sharp frontier theorem and ass:positive-sign-radius give
\[
\epsilon^\dagger(P)=\kappa_0-V(\theta)>0.
\tag{6}
\]
Under ass:sign-lp-strong-regularity, the nonsingular active basis and strict complementarity persist in a neighborhood of $\theta$. The reduced LP value is therefore affine there, with gradient equal to its unique equality-dual optimizer $y_*$. Combining this local envelope identity with (2) and (6),
\begin{align*}
\sqrt n(\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-\epsilon^\dagger)
&=-\sqrt n\{V(\widehat\theta)-V(\theta)\}+o_P(1)\\
&=-y_*^\top n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\psi_\theta(W_r)+o_P(1)
\Rightarrow N(0,\sigma^2).
\tag{7}
\end{align*}
The positive-part map is locally the identity in (6), which is why no boundary-normal mixture appears. The variance condition makes the limit nondegenerate. Training-sample consistency, fixed dimension, overlap, and convergence of the unique dual imply that the cross-fitted estimated scores converge in $L^2(P)$ to $-y_*^\top\psi_\theta$; their empirical second moment therefore converges to $\sigma^2$. If $z_{1-\alpha/2}$ is the standard-normal quantile,
\[
\left[\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-z_{1-\alpha/2}\widehat\sigma/\sqrt n,
\widehat\epsilon^\dagger+z_{1-\alpha/2}\widehat\sigma/\sqrt n\right]\cap[0,\infty)
\tag{8}
\]
has coverage tending to $1-\alpha$; the intersection is asymptotically inactive because (6) is strict.

For the nonregular branch, on the relative interior of the sign-feasible right-hand-side domain, lem:polyhedral-lp-value-calculus gives
\[
V'_\theta(h)=\min_{(y,\nu)\in\mathcal D^*(\theta)}y^\top h,
\tag{9}
\]
where $\mathcal D^*(\theta)$ is the reduced optimal-dual face. Thus $V$ is Hadamard directionally differentiable even when (9) is nonlinear. The chain rule for the scalar positive part gives
\[
\Phi'_\theta(h)=
\begin{cases}
-V'_\theta(h),&\kappa_0>V(\theta),\\
0,&\kappa_0<V(\theta),\\
\max\{-V'_\theta(h),0\},&\kappa_0=V(\theta).
\end{cases}
\tag{10}
\]
Let $\xi_1,\ldots,\xi_n$ be i.i.d. $N(0,1)$ multipliers independent of the data and put
\[
\mathbb G_n^*=n^{-1/2}\sum_{r=1}^n\xi_r
(\widehat\psi_{\theta,r}-\overline{\widehat\psi}_\theta).
\tag{11}
\]
Conditional on the data, (11) is exactly Gaussian with the empirical score covariance, which converges to $\Sigma_\theta$; hence it consistently estimates $G$ in (2). By the Fang--Santos directional delta and numerical-derivative resampling theorems, $s_n\downarrow0$ and $\sqrt ns_n\to\infty$ imply
\[
\frac{\Phi(\widehat\theta+s_n\mathbb G_n^*)-\Phi(\widehat\theta)}{s_n}
\ \leadsto_P\ \Phi'_\theta(G),
\tag{12}
\]
while $\sqrt n\{\Phi(\widehat\theta)-\Phi(\theta)\}\Rightarrow\Phi'_\theta(G)$. If $c_{1-\alpha}^*$ is the conditional $(1-\alpha)$ quantile of the absolute value of (12), then $[\widehat\epsilon^\dagger-c_{1-\alpha}^*/\sqrt n,\widehat\epsilon^\dagger+c_{1-\alpha}^*/\sqrt n]\cap[0,\infty)$ has limiting coverage $1-\alpha$ whenever the distribution of $|\Phi'_\theta(G)|$ is continuous at its $(1-\alpha)$ quantile (and the usual conservative continuity-point interpretation otherwise). Relative interiority of the sign-feasible domain is used to keep every local perturbation in a finite-valued LP domain. At its boundary, $R^-_\infty$ can disappear under arbitrarily small perturbations, changing $\overline S_-$ to $-\infty$ and $\epsilon^\dagger$ to $\infty$; no finite root-$n$ directional approximation can cover that discontinuity.
\end{proof}

sampling_model.design: i.i.d. draws W_1,\ldots,W_n from an observed law P\in\mathcal P_\epsilon
sampling_model.units: finite outcome, covariate, instrument, and resistance grids with a compatible structural law Q
sampling_model.overlap: positive instrument probability in every covariate cell, with each cutpoint represented by an instrument arm
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: The open inference question cross-fits conditional cell probabilities and thresholds, then derives rather than assumes cutpoint stability and dual regularity.

bibliography.citation: Jiyuan Tan, Jose Blanchet, and Vasilis Syrgkanis (2026). Partial Identification of Policy-Relevant Treatment Effects with Instrumental Variables via Optimal Transport. arXiv:2604.12263.
Yanqin Fan, Emmanuel Guerre, and Dongming Zhu (2017). Partial Identification of Functionals of the Joint Distribution of Potential Outcomes. Journal of Econometrics 197(1), 42-59.
Philip Marx (2024). Sharp Bounds in the Latent Index Selection Model. Journal of Econometrics 238(2), 105561.
Sergio Firpo and Geert Ridder (2019). Partial Identification of the Treatment Effect Distribution and Its Functionals. Journal of Econometrics 213, 210-234.

Brigham R. Frandsen and Lars J. Lefgren (2021). Partial Identification of the Distribution of Treatment Effects with an Application to the Knowledge Is Power Program. Quantitative Economics 12(1), 143-171.
Daniel Ober-Reynolds (2023). Estimating Functionals of the Joint Distribution of Potential Outcomes with Optimal Transport. arXiv:2311.09435.
Zheng Fang and Andres Santos (2019). Inference on Directionally Differentiable Functions. Review of Economic Studies 86(1), 377-412.
Zijun Gao, Shu Ge, and Jian Qian (2025). Bridging Multiple Worlds: Multi-marginal Optimal Transport for Causal Partial-identification Problem. Proceedings of AISTATS.
Sukjin Han and Shenshen Yang (2024). A Computational Approach to Identification of Treatment Effects for Policy Evaluation. Journal of Econometrics 240(1), 105680.
Matthew A. Masten and Alexandre Poirier (2020). Inference on Breakdown Frontiers. Quantitative Economics 11(1), 41-111.

tldr: The focal gain-band contrast is the probability-weighted difference between two policies of an individual gain falling in a specified band. One finite response-type array represents every feasible joint potential-outcome law, with correctly oriented treated and untreated factual marginals; sharp endpoints are defined only on observed laws for which that array exists. The headline open-ended question asks whether a common-law constrained-OT construction characterizes the least relaxation of a calibrated potential-outcome rank-association floor that permits the gain-band policy ranking to reverse. This is a common-law frontier rather than a collection of separately optimized conditional bounds. A second open-ended question asks for orthogonal inference for the resulting sign radius.

project_justification.gap: Product-cost generalized-Roy PRTE methods do not supply a sharp common-law characterization for a nonseparable individual gain-band event or its dependence-robust policy-ranking boundary.
project_justification.niche: Policy comparisons may depend on the share shifted toward harmful, modest, or exceptional individual gains, where potential-outcome dependence cannot be removed by a marginal reduction.
project_justification.fill: The project gives a finite response-type constrained-OT construction and poses its common-law pasting and association-loss sign frontier as a concrete open-ended question, with dual-based inference as a second open-ended question.

related_work: Tan, Blanchet, and Syrgkanis (2026) is the direct generalized-Roy anchor: its product-cost reduction is recovered by the additive-cost proposition, but it does not construct the nonseparable common-law band frontier. Fan, Guerre, and Zhu (2017) provides the joint-potential-outcome functional setting; the proposed lemma adds discrete-IV generalized-Roy factual compatibility and pasting. Marx (2024), Han and Yang (2024), and Gao, Ge, and Qian (2025) are nearby latent-index, policy-IV, and causal-OT formulations and are informative rather than claimed to be tightened. Firpo and Ridder (2019) and Frandsen and Lefgren (2021) show why individually sharp extrema cannot generally be assembled without a common coupling. Masten and Poirier (2020, Section 2) define and conduct inference for breakdown frontiers for binary-treatment conclusions under relaxations of random assignment and rank invariance; the present scalar association-loss radius instead indexes a pooled rank-association floor inside a finite discrete-IV generalized-Roy response-type polytope, so it is informed by rather than a direct extension of their observable-law frontier. Ober-Reynolds (2023) and Fang and Santos (2019) motivate the open regular-and-directional inference problem.
technical_internal_limitation: All sharpness claims are conditional on finite supports, saturated cutpoint coverage, both monotone-rank restrictions, and the calibrated pooled rank-association floor. The construction is not an identified-set result under unrestricted potential-outcome dependence.
honest_scope: Common-law sharpness, the association-loss sign frontier, root-n inference, dual-tie handling, and generated-cutpoint regularity are open-ended questions. The constructed endpoints are finite only on the displayed compatible observed-law domain. The K=1 lemma is the required factual-map sanity reduction, and the additive-cost proposition is solely a nesting check.
